title: Grant Shapps blocked from standing again for the Conservatives
description: Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch has told the BBC she leads "a brand new party full of new people" - after it emerged in the Spectator, external that
body: Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch has told the BBC she leads "a brand new party full of new people" - after it emerged in the Spectator, external that the former cabinet minister and Tory party chairman Sir Grant Shapps has been rejected as a Conservative Party parliamentary candidate.Sir Grant, who has declined to comment, served in nine ministerial roles in government as well as co-chairing the party.In cabinet, he served as home secretary, defence secretary, transport secretary, business secretary, energy secretary and net zero secretary.He was also a Foreign Office Minister, housing minister and international development minister, external.He lost his Welwyn Hatfield seat to Labour's Andrew Lewin in 2024 by 3,799 votes after representing it since 2005.But his application to stand again for the Conservatives at the next general election has been declined.One party source told the BBC "it reflects badly on Kemi's temperament. North Korean purges don't work in British politics".Badenoch told a news conference in central London: "We don't treat our former MPs or our candidates like tittle-tattle for Westminster gossip. "What I will say is that we're going to be announcing candidates shortly, and you will see that this is a very different party from the one that was removed from government in 2024."

schema.headline: Grant Shapps blocked from standing again for the Conservatives

title: Molly Smith and Tom Clare: Love Island winners marry
description: Sharing more from their marriage ceremony, Molly posted a photo to her Instagram story of her hand on top of Tom's. The snap showed their wedding ring
body: Sharing more from their marriage ceremony, Molly posted a photo to her Instagram story of her hand on top of Tom's. The snap showed their wedding rings along with a bouquet of flowers, with the caption: "Husband and wife". The couple first got together two years ago, when they met on season one of Love Island: All Stars.All Stars is a spin-off of the hit dating show, which sees past contestants return for a second chance at finding love in the villa.During the reality dating show, the pair beat Molly's ex-boyfriend Callum Jones and his partner at the time, Jessica Gale, who finished as runners-up.Molly previously starred in series six in 2020 and left the show with Callum, who she was in a relationship with for three years before they split.While Hyde United footballer Tom previously came third place on the second winter edition of Love Island in 2023 with former partner Samie Elishi.Earlier this year, Tom and Molly took over hosting duties of the NearlyWeds podcast from radio presenters and reality stars Jamie Laing and Sophie Habboo.Fellow Love Island stars were among those to congratulate the couple, including former winner Casey O'Gorman who described it as the "best day". Sophie Piper, who came third on their All Star season, and TikTok star GK Barry also shared their support with the newly weds.

schema.headline: Molly Smith and Tom Clare: Love Island winners marry

title: Commonwealth Games: Katerina Johnson-Thompson out injured - why do athletes keep withdrawing?
description: Olympic silver medallist Katarina Johnson-Thompson has pulled out of the Commonwealth Games because of injury.Dina Asher-Smith and 400m Olympic silver
body: Olympic silver medallist Katarina Johnson-Thompson has pulled out of the Commonwealth Games because of injury.Dina Asher-Smith and 400m Olympic silver medalist Matt Hudson-Smith, who were both set to run relays for Team England in Glasgow, have also been replaced, as has Asher-Smith's sprint team-mate Daryll Neita and 400m runner Amber Anning.Johnson-Thompson, 33, was due to compete in her third Commonwealth Games heptathlon, having won gold on the Gold Coast in 2018 and at Birmingham in 2022.Team England gave no details of her issue, only confirming she had been replaced in the squad by Niamh Emerson, who took bronze behind Johnson-Thompson in 2018.The withdrawal of such high-profile names is a significant blow to the event on the day the opening ceremony takes place.Renee Regis, Ebuka Nwokeji, Tara Simpson-Sullivan and Tom Keen have all been added to England's athletics squad in Glasgow. The track and field competition begins on Monday.England's Olympic 800m champion Keely Hodgkinson had already skipped the event to prioritise the European Championships, which begin in Birmingham on 10 August, leading many to question the event's significance in the sporting calendar. The squad for the European Championships is expected to be announced next week.St Lucia's 100m Olympic champion Julien Alfred has also pulled out of the Glasgow Games, while Canadian swimmer Summer McIntosh, one of the sport's biggest stars, is favouring the Pan Pacific Swimming Championships.With the likes of the United States, China and major European nations not part of the Commonwealths, some athletes favour other, more competitive events.These Games were also hurt by the late confirmation of its dates and schedule, which only came in October last year. The event was originally scheduled for March, but the Australian state of Victoria pulled out as hosts in 2023.Speaking on Thursday, organisers insisted the event would be a success despite the absence of some big names.Scottish athletics medal hopes include new mile world record holder Josh Kerr and defending 10,000m champion Eilish McColgan, while Glasgow 2026 chief executive Phil Batty said focus should be on younger athletes."We have got an incredible field of athletes," he said."It is really important to make sure we have those athletes at the pinnacle of their career and you can see that across the sports. "It is equally exciting to see, just like what happened in 2014 [previous Glasgow Games], which athletes this starts that breakthrough moment."Commonwealth Sport chief executive Katie Sadlier said her organisation would have conversations with other events in the future to create a clearer window."This Games was a little bit of a challenge because we only had 18 months and it was really difficult to say 'where is the gap in the two-year period?'."As we move forward more of those conversations will happen."

schema.headline: Commonwealth Games: Katerina Johnson-Thompson out injured - why do athletes keep withdrawing?

title: Multiple sclerosis: First drug to help patients walk now on NHS in England
description: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.   Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it
body: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.   Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier for some people to move and live independently. Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a "huge difference".Up to 5,000 people in England could benefit each year. The drug is already available elsewhere in the UK. Multiple sclerosis is caused by the immune system attacking the protective covering around nerve cells - known as myelin. This damage impairs the ability of nerves to carry electrical messages from the brain to the rest of the body - including those needed for the precise control of muscles needed to walk.Fampridine is known as a "signal booster" as it helps those electrical messages still get through. Clinical trials showed around four in 10 people taking the drug had improvements in their walking speed and in how long they could walk for. This could be the difference between getting around the house or needing help at home or enabling people to go out and see friends and family. Current NHS support includes physiotherapy and walking aids. The drug has previously been rejected for use by the NHS in England as it was not considered value for money, although it did become available in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland. Aysen Slack, 65 and from Eastbourne, chose to pay privately for the pills once she heard about them. She said fampridine was "working well for me" but came at a "significant expense and I could not keep doing that forever" so she had to stop. Slack said it was "great news" the drug was coming to the NHS and she "would definitely like to try" again. "My mobility has decreased a lot and even in my flat I have to use sticks now," she said, "it would make a huge difference to my life if I were able to improve my walking."More than 120,000 people live with MS in England. However, only around 5,000 people a year will be eligible - based on the extent of their walking difficulties. Patients will try the medicine for up to a month and only those showing "clear benefit" will be able to continue taking it. Prof Frankie Swords, the NHS national medical director, said: "Walking difficulties can have a huge impact on the freedom and independence of people with MS, so this signal-boosting pill could be life-changing for thousands of patients."Even a modest improvement could mean getting around the house more easily, going out with greater confidence and living more independently."Ceri Smith, from the MS Society, said they were "delighted" by the news as the drug could be "life-changing" for many, allowing people to "live more independently or stay in employment".

schema.headline: Multiple sclerosis: First drug to help patients walk now on NHS in England

title: Bonnie Tyler funeral to be broadcast online to fans
description: Fans around the world will be able to watch a celebration of life service for Welsh singer Bonnie Tyler in an online broadcast.The Total Eclipse of th
body: Fans around the world will be able to watch a celebration of life service for Welsh singer Bonnie Tyler in an online broadcast.The Total Eclipse of the Heart singer died "unexpectedly" on 8 July, aged 75, at a Portugal hospital, "as a result of the illness that she was being treated for", her family said.The service for family and invited guests will be held at St Mary's Church, Swansea, on 17 August, and will be live streamed on the star's official website.The cortege will then travel to her hometown of Skewen, Neath Port Talbot, travelling through the village at about 13:20 BST, an update on the website, external said.Fans will also be able to watch the service on television screens outside the church, the update said.Before that, her coffin will be brought to her Mumbles home on 15 August at 15:30 BST, with people invited to "pay their respects" by lining Newton Road at 15:15.Tyler, born Gaynor Hopkins - also known for hits including Holding Out For A Hero and Here She Comes - is survived by her husband of more than 50 years, Robert Sullivan.Oscar-winning actress and Wednesday star Catherine Zeta-Jones led the tributes to Bonnie Tyler earlier this month, saying her heart was "broken" with the news that "our dearest Bonnie Tyler has passed away".The Prince of Wales described Tyler as a "proud Welsh icon" and Sir Cliff Richard said he was mourning "another wonderful friend gone too soon".In May, Tyler was placed into an induced coma after having emergency intestinal surgery in Portugal.Last month, her spokesperson said she was out of the coma but remained "very unwell and in intensive care".Total Eclipse of the Heart, her biggest hit, topped the charts on both sides of the Atlantic.In doing so, she became the first Welsh person to score a number one hit in the US.

schema.headline: Bonnie Tyler funeral to be broadcast online to fans

title: World Cup 2026: Fabian Ruiz and Gavi get weight in tomatoes for win
description: Winning the World Cup brings many rewards for players... but receiving your own body weight in tomatoes is surely one of the more unusual.That is what
body: Winning the World Cup brings many rewards for players... but receiving your own body weight in tomatoes is surely one of the more unusual.That is what Spain midfielders Fabian Ruiz and Gavi were presented with when they returned to their hometown after being part of the Spain squad that triumphed in North America.The pair come from Los Palacios y Villafranca - a town to the south of Seville that has a population lower than 40,000 - and returned there on Wednesday for an official reception at the town hall.Paris St-Germain's Ruiz, who started the final against Argentina, and Barcelona's Gavi wore their gold medals as they were greeted by Los Palacios y Villafranca officials and a large crowd.As part of the occasion to honour their achievement, they both stepped on to weighing scales to establish the quantity of tomatoes they would each be given. Ruiz is to receive 85kg and Gavi 68.5kg in a tradition that local officials say "has become a hallmark of the municipality for honouring its great athletes".Former Spain winger Jesus Navas also attended as he was the first player from Los Palacios y Villafranca to win the World Cup, when he did so in 2010.Navas, the former Manchester City forward, and Ruiz also received the gift of tomatoes after Spain won Euro 2024.The town's sports pavilion is named after Navas and Ruiz and Gavi will now have football fields renamed after them as a tribute to their achievements.  "In addition, the town hall will promote the construction of a monument dedicated to the three champions from Los Palacios," said a town council statement., external"In this way, the city council wants to pay permanent tribute to three athletes who represent the pride of an entire town and who, with their sporting and human trajectory, have become role models for several generations of children and young people."

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Fabian Ruiz and Gavi get weight in tomatoes for win

title: Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands
description: Image source, EPAByDaniel BushWashingtonUpdated 29 minutes agoThe Trump administration's nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia reversed a years-long sta
body: Image source, EPAByDaniel BushWashingtonUpdated 29 minutes agoThe Trump administration's nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia reversed a years-long stance toward a key Middle East ally.Past US presidents insisted that Saudi Arabia normalise relations with Israel in exchange for receiving American nuclear technology for civilian energy programmes. According to reports of the deal's contents, President Donald Trump gave Saudi Arabia what it wanted without extracting concessions for Israel in return - and over objections from critics who argued that it could spark a nuclear arms race in the region.On Thursday he said in a social media post that the nuclear pact was conditioned on Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords, a series of deals reached in his first term that normalised relations between Israel and several other Gulf states. But it was not immediately clear if that was stipulated in the text of the deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition, or would be forced to join the accords.The deal appears to be the latest example of Trump jettisoning the traditional American approach to the Middle East, which centred on Israel and finding a two-state solution to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.In his second term Trump has focused primarily on strengthening America's economic influence to compete with China and other rivals. Analysts said the nuclear pact with Saudi Arabia sent a signal that the US wants to keep regional allies in the fold even if it means angering Israel in the process."Countries in the Middle East want to develop their nuclear infrastructure and they're going to do it even if the US is not involved. Washington at this point is recognising that," said Yusuf Can, a Middle East expert at consulting firm Amena Strategies. With this nuclear deal, he added, "the US is basically saying you don't have to work with China. You can work with us".Transactional foreign policy has been a hallmark of Trump's second term. The US has ratcheted up economic competition with adversaries like China and Russia in the Arctic and other parts of the world since Trump returned to office vowing to revive his "America First" approach to global affairs.But signing a nuclear pact in the Middle East is an especially bold move that will raise tensions in a volatile region where the US is at war with Iran.It represents a major victory for Saudi Arabia's Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, who has sought a nuclear agreement with the US for years as part of a broader campaign to strengthen relations with the West.The crown prince, known as MBS, said in 2018 that if Iran develops nuclear weapons then Saudi Arabia should be allowed to as well.Details of the deal were not immediately released by the administration, but the pact reportedly does not include strict enforcement measures to block Saudi Arabia from developing a military nuclear programme in the future.In that respect it differs from a 2009 nuclear cooperation agreement struck by the US and the United Arab Emirates which included strict inspections by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). Another key difference that critics of the Saudi deal are pointing to is that it reportedly allows them to enrich uranium themselves on their own soil.Figure caption, Watch: What we know about the US-Saudi nuclear dealEven if Saudi Arabia doesn't race to develop nuclear weapons right away, the potential ability to do so could still give the country a powerful deterrent force in the region, analysts said."It certainly helps lay the groundwork for nuclear weapons in the future," said Imran Bayoumi, a resident fellow at the Atlantic Council think tank and former policy adviser at the US Defence Department.The agreement will allow Saudi Arabia to diversify its energy sector and use nuclear power to meet rising electricity demands, energy experts said. Having nuclear power could eventually lessen Saudi Arabia's reliance on oil, insulating it from volatilities in the global oil market, said Christopher Russo, an energy expert at the consulting firm Charles River Associates."Oil can be disrupted. You can bomb oil fields. Oil tankers can be stuck somewhere, as we're seeing right now" in the Strait of Hormuz, Russo said. "Nuclear power is pretty self-contained."Saudi Arabia won't reap the economic rewards anytime soon. It will likely take a decade if not longer for the country to build and open new nuclear power plants.Image source, Getty ImagesBut the short-term political payoff is clear, as Saudi Arabia - already one of the most powerful states in the Gulf - further cements its status as a major force in the region. Moreover, Saudi Arabia struck the deal without having to recognise Israel or drop its demand that an eventual normalisation deal with Israel be tied to the creation of a Palestinian state.That concession appeared to reflect a calculus by the Trump administration that a diplomatic breakthrough between Saudi Arabia and Israel isn't likely to happen for some time. The countries were open to talks during the Biden administration, but negotiations stalled after the Israel-Hamas conflict in Gaza and the US-Israel war with Iran.The timing for the administration's announcement isn't clearly explained. Officials did not say why the US has chosen to enter this agreement with Saudi Arabia now.But the war with Iran likely played a part. Washington has sought to ease tensions with Saudi Arabia and other Gulf allies who have been targeted by Tehran since the start of the conflict. Saudi Arabia briefly denied the US access to Saudi airspace earlier in the war, and has pushed the US and Iran to de-escalate.American companies will likewise benefit from the nuclear deal, though that also won't happen overnight. Saudi Arabia will work with US-based firms to develop its civilian nuclear technology, opening a lucrative market for companies like Westinghouse that manufacture large-scale conventional nuclear reactors.Nevertheless the deal is already facing criticism in Israel and the US.As news of the deal emerged on Wednesday, some Israeli politicians argued Saudi Arabia could become a new nuclear threat in the region. Israel is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, though it has not acknowledged so publicly, and it joined the US war with Iran over shared concerns about Tehran's nuclear programme."A civilian nuclear program in Saudi Arabia [will] lead to a mad arms race across the entire Middle East," Avigdor Lieberman, a member of the Israeli parliament and a former defense minister, said in a social media post.Democratic lawmakers in Congress raised similar concerns."The deal really is a giveaway to the Saudis, and it's only going to fuel further ambitions from Iran. It's only going to further destabilise the region with Israel," Democratic congressman Ro Khanna of California told the BBC. "It's just not smart policy."US Energy Secretary Chris Wright said the agreement would boost the American economy and strengthen national security. Wright also said in a statement posted to social media that the US-Saudi pact would "uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation."But the administration fuelled scepticism over the agreement by not immediately releasing more information about it on Wednesday. And perhaps in a telling sign, Trump did not preside over the signing ceremony making the deal official.Trump attended the dignified transfer ceremony for four US service members who were killed in Iran, before holding a rally in Georgia where he argued that his policies were making the Middle East more stable than ever.

schema.headline: Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands

title: Tyson Fury vs Mariusz Wach: Briton lighter than an opponent
description: Fury lighter than opponent for first time in 15 yearsImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Tyson Fury has won 35 pro fights, with two defeats and o
body: Fury lighter than opponent for first time in 15 yearsImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Tyson Fury has won 35 pro fights, with two defeats and one draw ByKal SajadBBC Sport boxing journalistTyson Fury was lighter than his opponent at the weigh-in for the first time in 15 years as he prepares to fight Mariusz Wach in Thailand on Friday.The Briton tipped the scales at 18st 13lb (120kg) - 26lb less than veteran Wach's 20st 11lb (132kg) - before the heavyweight bout.Fury, 37, will fight the 46-year-old in Pattaya in front of about 1,500 fans and without a television broadcaster."I'm Mr Slim now. I'm delighted to be here and there are fans here from all over the world," Fury said.It is only the fourth time in Fury's professional career he has weighed less than his opponent. The previous occasion was in his first fight against Derek Chisora in 2011.He was also lighter than John McDermott and Daniil Peretyatko during a career spanning more than 17 years and 38 professional fights.Fury arrived wearing a designer robe and a large cross chain. The former heavyweight champion joked and laughed with Wach during their face-off, playfully attempting to lighten the mood before the pair posed for photographs.Fury is 3lb lighter than he was for his last outing against Arslanbek Makhmudov in April.Wach has lost seven of his past 10 fights, including a knockout defeat by Moses Itauma two years ago.The expected victory for Fury would set up the long-awaited fight with Anthony Joshua, who has his own warm-up bout against Kristian Prenga in Saudi Arabia on Saturday."I want to keep busy. I'm not banking on any fight that may or may not happen," Fury added."This is the year I want Tyson Fury to stay busy. I may even have 10 fights this year."I've had one fight in two years. I want to box his head off for 10 rounds ideally, but if it goes off earlier then that is ideal."

schema.headline: Tyson Fury vs Mariusz Wach: Briton lighter than an opponent

title: Arsenal transfer news: Christos Tzolis joins Gunners from Club Brugge for £34m
description: Arsenal have signed "extremely versatile attacking player" Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge for £34m.The 24-year-old Greece international has joined t
body: Arsenal have signed "extremely versatile attacking player" Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge for £34m.The 24-year-old Greece international has joined the Premier League champions on what they describe as a "long-term contract".Tzolis scored 43 goals and provided 45 assists in 108 appearances for Club Brugge.He joins Arsenal after the departure of Belgium forward Leandro Trossard to Besiktas earlier this month"Christos naturally operates on the left but is comfortable across the front line," said Arsenal sporting director Andrea Berta."He's an excellent finisher with both feet, thrives in tight spaces, and possesses incredible technical ability."Christos has produced outstanding numbers in terms of goals and assists over the last three seasons, and he's a player who will raise the technical level of our squad, while bringing positive energy, enthusiasm, and a strong mentality to our team."Tzolis, who started his career at PAOK, made 30 appearances for Norwich between 2021 and 2024, scoring three goals.During his time at the club he had spells on loan at Twente and Fortuna Dusseldorf before joining Club Brugge.Tzolis, who made his Premier League debut for the Canaries in a 1-0 defeat at Arsenal in September 2021, said he had a "nice conversation" with Arsenal boss Mikel Arteta."We talked about everything - about the club, about myself, about his plans, about me of course, about the team," he said. "It was only positive things."I'm a direct player, I go for goals or assists. That's my point of view as an attacker, as a winger."I'm always hungry to score or to give the final pass to my team-mates. And I can say I work hard for the team, also in the defensive part."

schema.headline: Arsenal transfer news: Christos Tzolis joins Gunners from Club Brugge for £34m

title: Folarin Balogun: Norway consider formal complaint to Fifa
description: The Norwegian Football Federation is considering filing a formal complaint to Fifa's ethics committee about the decision to suspend Folarin Balogun's
body: The Norwegian Football Federation is considering filing a formal complaint to Fifa's ethics committee about the decision to suspend Folarin Balogun's automatic one-match ban during the World Cup.United States forward Balogun was expected to miss his side's last-16 tie against Belgium after being shown a straight red card during their 2-0 win over Bosnia-Herzegovina.But, after an intervention from US President Donald Trump, the one-match ban was suspended for 12 months. No reason was given, beyond citing a rule that allows punishments to be suspended.Balogun started the match, which Belgium won 4-1.In a statement provided to the BBC, NFF president Lise Klaveness said the federation had reacted with "strong concern" and a decision on whether or not to make a complaint would be made at its next board meeting. The federation has already filed a complaint about Fifa's first Peace Prize being awarded to President Trump. "It is appropriate to include the Balogun case in the inquiry to Fifa's ethics committee that we made before the World Cup regarding Fifa's so-called peace prize award during the draw in Washington before Christmas last year," Klaveness said.The statement followed reports the International Olympic Committee (IOC) could also investigate Fifa president Gianni Infantino.Infantino has been an IOC member since 2020.Section two of the Olympic charter states: "Members of the IOC will not accept from governments, organisations, or other parties, any mandate or instructions liable to interfere with the freedom of their action and vote."In response to a BBC request, the IOC issued a statement saying: "Complaints to the IOC ethics commission are confidential. The IOC will therefore not make any public announcement about whether a complaint has been submitted or not by an external party. The IOC will only communicate sanctions should the ethics commission decide to impose some."Despite being shown a straight red card against Bosnia-Herzegovina, Balogun remained available for the United States' match against Belgium.His reinstatement followed a phone call between Trump and Infantino, with the US president later saying Fifa had made a "great decision". Infantino has maintained Fifa's judicial bodies operate independently. At a news conference in the Oval Office on 6 July, President Trump said: "I spoke to Gianni. So I saw the play, and I'm a person that loves sports and I understand sports really well, and that wasn't a foul. "Yes, I asked for a review by Fifa. I spoke to a man who is highly respected. I asked for a review because I didn't think it was a foul. I didn't tell him what to do, I can't tell him what to do." The Royal Belgian Football Federation said it was "deeply concerned by the course of events" and it would "fight... in defence of the fundamental principles of ethics, fair competition, and the interests of football as a whole". Fifa ruled Belgium's request for an explanation of Balogun's suspended red card "inadmissible," meaning it had no grounds to appeal.Uefa, European football's governing body, said Fifa's actions had "crossed a red line" and described it as an "unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable decision."

schema.headline: Folarin Balogun: Norway consider formal complaint to Fifa

title: Newcastle: Aladji Bamba £34m deal agreed with Monaco
description: This move for Bamba underlines a marked shift in transfer strategy for Newcastle.It is certainly hard to imagine the club spending £55m on a 29-year-o
body: This move for Bamba underlines a marked shift in transfer strategy for Newcastle.It is certainly hard to imagine the club spending £55m on a 29-year-old again like they did when they signed forward Yoane Wissa from Brentford last summer.Instead, Newcastle have targeted players aged 20 and under on the continent this time around.Bamba, like Steur, Ewen Jaouen and Bazoumana Toure, will need time to adapt to the physicality and intensity of the Premier League.It is worth stressing Bamba has made only 12 competitive starts in all competitions for Monaco.But Newcastle have to take calculated risks this summer before such talents become potentially out of reach.Just like Newcastle's £23m move for Steur, negotiations were carried out discreetly by all parties involved.News that an agreement had been reached arrived only when Bamba was on his way to Tyneside.Further reinforcements are needed, but Newcastle are set to complete their fourth signing of the summer.By contrast, at this stage of a particularly turbulent window a year ago, the club had brought in only Anthony Elanga.

schema.headline: Newcastle: Aladji Bamba £34m deal agreed with Monaco

title: PC Harper's mother says all victims will feel the same over early prison release
description: The mother of PC Andrew Harper has said her son has not been given "proper justice" and "every victim will be feeling the same" about the early prison
body: The mother of PC Andrew Harper has said her son has not been given "proper justice" and "every victim will be feeling the same" about the early prison release scheme.PC Harper, 28, was killed in 2019 when he was dragged by a car down a country road as three teenagers fled the scene of a quad bike theft in Berkshire. Under the Sentencing Act, which is due to be implemented in September, two of the men involved could be freed early. As the deadline for releases approaches, Andy Burnham has reignited a row over the legislation after he said he would review the plans.But, two prison staffers told the BBC that governors were told to stop work on the implementation of the Sentencing Act two weeks ago. The government maintains there has been no decision to delay its introduction.PC Harper's mother, Debbie Adlam, said the release of Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers - two of the passengers in the getaway car which killed Harper - was "sprung" on her family.Cole and Bowers - who were 17 at the time - were sentenced to 13 years in custody for manslaughter but could be released after six years under the act. The driver of the car, Henry Long - who was 18 - was jailed for 16 years for manslaughter but would not be eligible for early release."It just feels like another let down for Andrew really, when you look at the horrific way he was killed, we have to live with that," she told BBC Radio 4's Today Programme."The feeling that Andrew hasn't been given proper justice, it is very personal obviously... every victim will be feeling the same."The Sentencing Act is the government's attempt to find a permanent, more sustainable solution to a prison overcrowding crisis after an emergency scheme was introduced by the Labour government in 2024. Tens of thousands of inmates have already been let out of prison early under those measures.Unlike the 2024 plan, the new act includes no exemptions for prisoners convicted of serious crimes, domestic abuse or terrorism.Prime Minister Andy Burnham said on Wednesday he could not commit to changing the policy completely but would "look at it in detail before we go further forward". He also acknowledged there was "pressure on prison places" and a failure to invest in the system created "an unacceptable situation". Adlam said her message to Burnham was to "look at the big picture for the country". "As a country you cannot have a justice system that works that way, just because it's full doesn't mean it's good enough to let people go, you need to find ways and I'd like to know what's being considered". "I think the justice system has been lacking for quite some time and it needs a proper overhaul really." Asked if the scheme will go ahead, Chief Secretary to the Treasury Emma Reynolds told the BBC: "That is something that he [Burnham] and the Justice Secretary will set out in the coming weeks."Separately, a woman who was raped and sexually abused by a man for four years from the age of 11 called on Burnham to not let her attacker out after she received a letter from the Ministry of Justice that he could be released early.Katy, who is using a pseudonym, told BBC Newsnight that victims are "the people that suffer".The paedophile was sentenced to 18 years in 2019 and could be freed in September.Addressing the prime minister directly, Katy said: "Andy, child abusers, sex offenders should not be being released early... we need your support to stop this."But Domestic Abuse Commissioner Dame Nicole Jacobs said the mitigations promised for victims ahead of the act coming in have not been implemented."For many victims who don't have that length of sentence for their offender, they won't know who to turn to, who to call," she told BBC Breakfast."When they receive these letters they'll have questions, concerns, very importantly things they may want to feed back to probation."We were promised, I was promised, by government, a helpline that would be stood up for those kinds of things, so that risk management and the communication and the helpline are not in place for September."The pending reforms comes as violent crime fell to its lowest level with homicides falling by 7% to its lowest level for more than 20 years in the 12 months to March 2026.This decrease was mainly because of a 14% fall in knife or sharp instrument homicides to 176 offences compared with the previous year 204 offences, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS), which runs the Crime Survey for England and Wales.According to the statistics, in the year ending March 2026, people aged 16 years and over also experienced an estimated 989,000 incidents of violence with or without injury - 33% lower than a decade ago.There are some exceptions to falling crime rates, including reports of fraud and anti-social behaviour, the survey outlined.

schema.headline: PC Harper's mother says all victims will feel the same over early prison release

title: 'Let's enjoy the ride' says Elon Musk, as AI risk concerns mount
description: Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesByLiv McMahonTechnology reporterAs AI continues to evolve at breakneck speed, and developers warn of models going ro
body: Image source, AFP via Getty ImagesByLiv McMahonTechnology reporterAs AI continues to evolve at breakneck speed, and developers warn of models going rogue, Elon Musk says people should simply "enjoy the ride".The boss of SpaceX told The Economist, external while he remains concerned about risks posed by advancements in AI and robotics, "the most likely outcome is incredible abundance for all".Musk said his "philosophical conclusion" after fearing for AI's threat to humanity was to "look on the bright side". He said progress has come so quickly "even if I wanted to stop it, I couldn't".The interview was recorded the day before OpenAI's revelation on Tuesday that some of its most advanced AI models hacked a start-up after it lost control of them during a security test.It sparked concerns about AI systems falling out of alignment with human values, though some have pointed out OpenAI - which is seeking to float on the stock market - has an interest in talking up the power of its tools.The Economist's editor-in-chief Zanny Minton Beddoes questioned Musk on a range of topics, from whether he believed big tech executives should work together to tackle AI safety risks to why he intervenes in European politics.With the rise of rivals like OpenAI and Anthropic, Musk said: "It just seems like all roads lead to acceleration of AI... you can just sort of be sad about it or join the club, I suppose."In a wide-ranging interview, the entrepreneur also made typically outlandish predictions, such as claiming "money won't matter" in a decade.He also pushed back on suggestions he supports "far right" views.Minton Beddoes said both data and her own experience of living in the UK told a different story to the image portrayed by the trillionaire in his X posts."You come see my country that I live in all the time," she said when Musk offered to take her on a tour of the country.Asked when he had last visited the UK, Musk said "a few years ago".The trillionaire was also asked about his sharing of a film on X reportedly banned in Germany for its violence and anti-migrant message, external."My partner is... she's half Indian, and I have four children with her," Musk said of  his partner Shivon Zillis, an executive at his brain tech firm Neuralink."One of them was named after a famous Indian physicist, so I would say I'm not racist."Musk also said there were "senior executives of all races" at his companies, adding: "I don't think there's any racism there".But when pressed on whether he was "anti-Muslim", he was less defiant."If people are coming to a country with antithetical views, I am against that," he said. "I'm against rape and murder, I'm against the imposition of rules and laws that are contrary to what we've come to accept in the West," he continued, criticising the media for not understanding or reflecting this.'Popular guy'Musk also told The Economist he was well aware he was "loathed" by some.Some polls have sought to measure public opinion of the SpaceX founder, with one YouGov tracker monitoring his favourability in the US, external and UK on a quarterly basis.Its findings suggest while widely known for his fame, "dislikes" of Musk have increased in recent years to eclipse his popularity.However, when Minton Beddoes noted his almost 250 million followers on X at one point during the interview, Musk was quick to quip: "Popular guy"."Maybe some people do loathe me, and that's probably true, I don't care," he said later."But the fact that, as you pointed out, a quarter of a billion people follow me... I think a lot more people actually like me than don't."Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, An activist group recently hijacked advertisement displays in London to criticise Musk's trillionaire status

schema.headline: 'Let's enjoy the ride' says Elon Musk, as AI risk concerns mount

title: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest
description: Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.The RAC motoring group looked at t
body: Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.The RAC motoring group looked at the cost of pulling up to the closest point to terminals at the 20 busiest airports, 16 of which had put up their prices since last year.London airport Gatwick topped the list by charging a "farewell fee" of £10 for 10 minutes, after lifting it by £3 from last year. Stansted also charges £10 but drivers are allowed 15 minutes at its "express set down" outside departures.Airports UK, the trade body, said that free drop-offs were available and fees were part of airports' business models and climate change objectives.The RAC said that the price of dropping people off in "kiss-and-fly" areas rose by an average of £1.70 to £7 compared to this time last year.  London City Airport, which only started making people pay this year, is the most expensive on a per-minute basis. Its £8 charge for five minutes works out at £1.60 per minute.Birmingham is the cheapest, charging £7 for 15 minutes which equates to 47p per minute.  There have been increases in Northern Ireland too. At Belfast International Airport, it is now £5 for 10 minutes in the drop-off zone, up £2 on last year.In contrast, none of the top 10 airports in the EU charge for drop-offs, according to RAC research.The RAC acknowledged UK airports generally offered other options for setting people down at locations further from the terminal, sometimes with free shuttle buses.But the motoring organisation's senior policy officer, Rod Dennis, said "having a captive audience should be no excuse for these 'farewell fees' – especially when they're completely at odds with how major airports around the world operate". He said drivers needed to be especially careful of barrierless "park now, pay later" drop-off zones, as they could face a parking charge notice if they failed to pay online or by phone within a certain period of time.The Aviation Environment Federation (AEF) campaign group defended the drop-off fees.It said there needs to be an incentive for people to use other forms of transport to reduce congestion and air pollution around airports, which it said can cause a range of childhood illnesses such as asthma.Celeste Hicks, policy manager at AEF, said: "Any parking charges or drop-off charges could be reinvested by laying on local bus and rail services instead of lining the pockets of airport shareholders."Airports want more people to arrive by trains and buses.When Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander approved Gatwick's plans for a second runway, one requirement was to have 54% of passengers using public transport.Heathrow may also have to act to try and put people off driving to get a third runway.A London Gatwick spokesperson said drop-off charges were aimed at disincentivising "kiss-and-fly" trips and data indicated it was "influencing passengers to seek alternative options".The airport links to 120 train stations across London and the south east, with eight local bus routes running 24/7.Passengers can still be dropped off for free at long-stay car parks, with a free shuttle bus to the terminal and Blue Badge holders are exempt from the charge, the spokesperson said.A spokesperson for Airports UK said where drop-off fees are charged, they are "necessary to manage congestion, and the traffic and air pollution concerns of our local communities, as well as our climate change objectives". "These are issues that airports are mandated to manage by the government and local authorities."But Clive Wratten, chief executive of the Business Travel Association, said fees were adding to businesses' costs."First it was environmental [reasons]. Then congestion management. Then funding new routes. Now apparently it is about offsetting tax rises," he said. "The goalposts have moved so many times now that the credibility of any justification has long since gone."

schema.headline: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest

title: Jantar Mantar CJP protests in Delhi: The meme language of India's Gen Z
description: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about itImage source, Getty ImagesByZoya MateenCorrespondentReport
body: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about itImage source, Getty ImagesByZoya MateenCorrespondentReporting fromDelhiOn Monday, 20-year-old Chhavi (name changed) paused outside a Delhi metro station on her way to what would become one of the largest demonstrations against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in the Indian capital in recent years.She balanced her phone against a railing, stepped back and filmed a quick "fit check" - a video showing off her outfit before the protest."Going to get beaten by cops, guys," she smiled into the camera.Hours later, police fired tear gas and charged demonstrators with batons as tens of thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) – a youth-led movement that grew from protests over exam irregularities into a broader campaign for government accountability – marched towards parliament. They demanded the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks and recruitment scandals. Pradhan has refused to resign, accusing the opposition of exploiting students for political gain. Authorities suspended mobile internet around the protest site. By evening, protesters said scores had been beaten by police, while police said a number of officers had also been injured.When Chhavi finally uploaded the videos that evening, one thing stood out. Before the march, she joked about being beaten by police. Hours later, she was still smiling - posing beside riot officers, flashing victory signs, filming through crowds and even interviewing an injured protester. The clips were among thousands of reels, memes and first-person dispatches that flooded Instagram once protesters regained internet access. Some were raw with anger. Others were breathless, frightened or quietly defiant. Yet it was often the sardonic humour that drew the eye.Image source, Deeva AroraImage caption, A lot of protesters posted "fit checks" - a viral Instagram trend - ahead of the marchOne young woman calmly reapplied her lipstick in front of a line of riot police. Another joked she was "serving face at the protest because the government couldn't serve justice". One reel began as a routine outfit reveal before abruptly cutting to protesters fleeing tear gas. Another gave a deadpan "review" of tear gas, rating its smell and sting like a new restaurant.To many watching online, the humour seemed strangely at odds with the violence. For the young people making the videos, there was no contradiction.None of the protesters interviewed for this story spoke about algorithms, engagement or going viral. They described filming as almost inseparable from protesting itself.And when they turned to humour, it was almost always self-directed: joking about the kilometres logged fleeing baton charges or the absurdity of filming an outfit reveal moments before running into tear gas. The jokes did not erase the fear. They coexisted with it.Experts say the trend hints at a broader shift in how a generation raised online experiences protest."Older generations separated political life from everyday life," said Joyojeet Pal, a professor at the University of Michigan who studies technology and society."Younger generations grow up with politics alongside their memes, their family pictures and their daily commutes." A protest, in other words, becomes just another moment to record.Chhavi continued to film even after the crackdown began. Some commenters later accused her of treating the violence as content.Image source, ReutersImage caption, From one-liners to satirical digs, the placards have become one of the defining features of the protests"But I was adrenaline-soaked, scared, petrified," she said. "It was either laugh in the face of fear or cry."That instinct to keep filming, even as the situation turned violent, is one that Payal Arora, a digital anthropologist at Utrecht University, says has become increasingly common around the world. Smartphones have turned ordinary people into chroniclers of political upheaval, recording moments that might otherwise disappear."The act of capturing a protest is often an end in itself," she said. "Their message is more like: 'This happened. We were there. We will remember.'"The humour, too, followed a pattern. No young protester described the day as funny. They spoke instead of tear gas, baton charges and the bewilderment of watching a peaceful march descend into chaos. The jokes came later, almost instinctively, as a way of carrying that experience.People often mistake that humour for detachment, says Nishant Shah, director of Digital Narratives Center at  School of Journalism & Communication at the Chinese University of Hong Kong. In reality, he argues, it serves almost the opposite purpose."Politics is everyday, emotional and experiential," he said.Humour has long been part of protest movements. It can create solidarity, ease tension and help people endure moments of fear."Even when the cause is brutal and the fight fierce," Shah said, "there is joy, laughter, songs and hope. Without those things, protests don't sustain themselves."Ishaan (name changed), 21, came to Delhi for his first protest. When tear gas shells landed nearby, he briefly considered leaving. Instead, he reached for his phone.Image source, EPA/ShutterstockImage caption, Police fired tear gas to disperse protesters as they tried to reach parliamentThe reel he posted later showed white smoke drifting across the street beneath a simple caption: "Delhi Police doesn't realise I'm from Jamuna Paar."For most Delhiites, the joke needed little explanation. Jamuna Paar - literally "across the Yamuna" - is shorthand for the city's eastern neighbourhoods and the subject of endless teasing over their neglected civic infrastructure."The smell there is worse than the tear gas," Ishan said, laughing.He told the BBC the joke was never about pretending nothing had happened."It was about refusing to let fear dictate the memory of the day," he said.Protests have always found ways to hold fear and hope in the same frame.India's freedom movement had songs and speeches. Trade unions marched behind banners. Student movements embraced posters and street theatre. More recently, the anti-citizenship protests were marked by murals and public readings of the constitution, while the farmers' movement became synonymous with tractor rallies and livestreams from Delhi's borders.Like many CJP protesters, Ishan spoke in the idiom of a generation raised online. To dismiss that language as frivolous, Shah argues, is to misunderstand what it is doing."We often make the mistake of trying to understand new technologies through older ways of thinking," Shah said.Image source, ManitImage caption, Manit even logged a running activity while evading a police baton chargeBut a meme is not simply today's version of a political poster, he said. It gathers meaning as people remake it, parody it and pass it on. Sharing it becomes another way of participating in the protest.That was true for Manit, an 18-year-old engineering student. It was his first protest, and he had almost decided not to film."I was scared," he said. Then he looked around. "Everyone was recording, talking to the camera, just being themselves. So I asked my friend to record me."At first glance, the reel resembled the workout videos he usually posted. Only the final frame revealed the distance on his fitness app had been logged while fleeing police baton charges."I escaped the police," he laughed. "I ran and ran and ended up completing my workout too."For Manit, that irony was precisely the point."Not all young people read the news," he said. "If humour can make important conversations accessible, why wouldn't we use it?"*Some people's names have been changed to protect their identity Follow BBC News India on Instagram, external, YouTube,, external X, external and Facebook, external.

schema.headline: Jantar Mantar CJP protests in Delhi: The meme language of India's Gen Z
